text: The surgeon prepares to start the intracorporeal anastomosis, having identified an ischemic zone on the specimen side.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy approximately three to four centimeters in size, preparing the opening to accommodate the anvil.
The anvil is placed into the abdomen through the right lower quadrant port site.
A gel port is placed in the right lower quadrant to seal the location, allowing for the extraction site and simultaneous use of robotic instruments.
The surgeon inserts the anvil into the collotomy, carefully advancing the spike as far proximal to the bowel as possible.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to secure the anvil in place, preventing it from being lost and allowing precise control over the exit point of the anvil and spike.
The surgeon uses Firefly and Imogene guidance to precisely direct the spike's exit location from the bowel, following the insertion of the anvil into the collotomy.
The surgeon ensures the V-lock suture is snug without loops, positioning the green anvil portion away from the staple line.

The surgeon pulls the anvil out using the V-lock suture. Once the anvil and V-lock are removed, the V-lock is used to close the colotomy on the specimen side and assist in removing the green anvil spike.
The surgeon uses the suture needle from the V-lock to remove the spike, inserting it in a specific manner and performing a quick pop-off maneuver.
